name: Cross-plan temple silhouette
description: Overall silhouette of a temple whose footprint forms a cross: a long rectangular nave running on the major axis is crossed by a shorter transept on the perpendicular axis. When the nave is markedly longer than the transept the plan reads as a Latin cross, the canonical Gothic cathedral form; when nave and transept are nearly equal it becomes a Greek cross, typical of Byzantine and Renaissance churches. The crossing — the square where the two arms meet — is the visual and spiritual center, usually emphasized vertically with a dome, lantern or tower that lifts the silhouette above the surrounding rooflines. The main entrance sits at the far west end of the nave, opposite the apse, and the transept ends offer secondary side entrances.
placement_rules: Footprint is two intersecting rectangles forming a cross; the nave runs along the long (east-west) axis and the transept along the perpendicular (north-south) axis.
Nave length:width ratio between 1.5 and 2.5 — shorter reads as a hall, longer as a basilica corridor.
Transept length:width ratio between 0.6 and 1.0 of the nave width — this keeps the cross legible without overpowering the nave.
The crossing (intersection square) must be emphasized vertically: place a dome, lantern, or central tower so the silhouette peaks at the center.
Main entrance at the far west end of the nave, facing the apse at the east end; secondary entrances may sit at the two transept ends.
Single tall storey — interior height of 8-15 blocks — so the vertical proportion dominates the horizontal footprint.
When nave length closely matches transept length the plan becomes a Greek cross (Byzantine/Renaissance); otherwise a Latin cross (Gothic).

gothic.notes: Tall narrow nave with steep ridge roof, central spire over the crossing, pointed-arch openings along the long walls; the transept ends carry rose-window-style large openings.

renaissance.notes: Balanced near-Greek-cross proportions, shallow gabled arms, large hemispherical dome on a drum over the crossing; pale stone walls with classical pilasters at the corners.
